title: The Steel Seizure Crisis
doctrineArea: Youngstown Framework / Separation of Powers
description: During an overseas military conflict, steelworkers threaten a nationwide strike. The President issues an executive order seizing the steel mills, claiming the action is necessary to sustain the war effort. No statute authorizes the seizure, and Congress previously considered but declined to grant such authority.

steps.prompt: Under Justice Jackson's concurrence in Youngstown, which category best describes the President's action?

choices.text: Category One (Zenith) — The President acts pursuant to congressional authorization, so power is at its maximum.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. Congress never authorized the seizure. In fact, Congress considered and declined to grant the President seizure authority, which is probative of the President's lack of power. This is not a case where the President acts with congressional backing.

choices.text: Category Two (Zone of Twilight) — The President acts in the absence of any congressional direction either way.
choices.explanation: Partially defensible but not the best answer. While the Zone of Twilight applies when Congress has neither authorized nor prohibited action, here Congress affirmatively considered and rejected granting this power. That congressional inaction is probative evidence of congressional opposition, pushing the analysis toward Category Three.

choices.text: Category Three (Lowest Ebb) — The President acts contrary to the expressed or implied will of Congress.
choices.explanation: Correct. Jackson classified the steel seizure as Category Three because Congress considered and declined to grant seizure authority. When the President acts contrary to Congress's will, presidential power is at its lowest ebb and can prevail only if the President invokes an exclusive, preclusive constitutional power.

choices.text: This is a political question the courts cannot decide.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. The Supreme Court decided this case 7–2 on the merits. The Youngstown framework exists precisely because the Court found this was a justiciable controversy about the limits of presidential power.

steps.prompt: The government argues the seizure is justified by the President's constitutional powers. Which of the following is the strongest basis for the President's claim?

choices.text: The Vesting Clause of Article II, which vests 'the executive power' in the President without the 'herein granted' limitation found in Article I.
choices.explanation: This is a real argument, but Jackson rejected it. He noted that reading the Vesting Clause to include all conceivable executive powers would be 'totalitarian' and would render the specific enumerated powers redundant — why list specific powers if everything is already included?

choices.text: The Commander in Chief Clause, because the seizure supports the military effort overseas.
choices.explanation: Jackson addressed this directly and found it insufficient. He wrote that allowing the Commander in Chief power to vastly enlarge presidential control over internal domestic affairs simply by linking it to an overseas conflict would be 'sinister and alarming.' Congress, not the President, has the power to support and maintain the armed forces.

choices.text: An inherent presidential emergency power to act when the nation faces urgent danger.
choices.explanation: Jackson rejected this most forcefully. He noted that the framers likely chose not to grant an emergency power because they 'suspected that emergency powers would tend to kindle emergencies.' The President has no freestanding inherent power to act in an emergency without statutory or constitutional authorization, especially in Category Three.

choices.text: None of the above — there is no constitutional basis sufficient to sustain the action in Category Three.
choices.explanation: Correct. Jackson systematically rejected the Vesting Clause, Commander in Chief, Take Care Clause, and inherent emergency powers as insufficient bases. In Category Three, only an exclusive, preclusive presidential power (like the pardon power) can prevail, and none of these claimed powers meets that threshold.

steps.prompt: As the deciding Justice, how do you rule on the constitutionality of the steel seizure?

choices.text: The seizure is constitutional — the President's war powers include the authority to secure materials essential to the military effort.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. The Supreme Court held 7–2 that the seizure was unauthorized. Neither the Commander in Chief power nor any other constitutional provision justified seizing private property domestically. The Take Care Clause requires laws to execute, and no law authorized this seizure.

choices.text: The seizure is unconstitutional — the executive order lacks statutory or constitutional authorization, and Congress's decision not to grant this power is probative evidence against the President.
choices.explanation: Correct. Justice Black's majority opinion held that an executive order must stem from an act of Congress or the Constitution itself. Congress considered and declined to grant seizure power. No constitutional provision — Commander in Chief, Take Care, or otherwise — justified the action. The seizure was unconstitutional.

choices.text: The seizure should be upheld under the President's historical practice of seizing property during wartime.
choices.explanation: This invokes Frankfurter's concurrence on historical practice. However, Frankfurter found that 'three isolated instances do not add up' to a systematic, unbroken executive practice sufficient to gloss executive power. The factors — number, scope, duration, and congressional acquiescence — were not satisfied here.

overallExplanation: Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. v. Sawyer (1952) is the foundational case for separation-of-powers analysis. Justice Jackson's three-category concurrence provides the starting framework for any clash between presidential and congressional power. Category One (Zenith): the President acts with congressional authorization — maximum power. Category Two (Zone of Twilight): the President acts without congressional direction — power depends on 'the imperatives of events and contemporary imponderables.' Category Three (Lowest Ebb): the President acts contrary to Congress's will — the President can prevail only by invoking an exclusive, preclusive constitutional power. The steel seizure fell into Category Three because Congress considered and rejected granting seizure authority. Jackson then systematically rejected the Vesting Clause, Commander in Chief, Take Care Clause, and inherent emergency powers. The case teaches that the Youngstown framework is the starting point of analysis but does not resolve the question on its own — it merely tells you where you are on the spectrum of presidential authority.
caseReferences: Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. v. Sawyer, 343 U.S. 579 (1952)
Justice Jackson's Concurrence (Three-Category Framework)
Justice Frankfurter's Concurrence (Historical Practice as Constitutional Gloss)
Justice Black's Majority Opinion (Statutory and Constitutional Authorization Required)
professorNote: Youngstown is the starting point of any separation-of-powers analysis — but it is only the starting point. It tells you where you are on the spectrum of presidential authority but does not resolve the question on its own. On an exam, always identify the Youngstown category first, then analyze the specific constitutional power claimed. Students frequently confuse Category Two (congressional silence) with Category Three (congressional opposition). The key signal for Category Three is evidence that Congress considered and declined to act — that is probative of congressional opposition, not mere silence.
otherSide: The strongest argument for the President is Frankfurter's historical-practice gloss: if presidents have repeatedly seized private property during wartime emergencies, and Congress has known about it and never objected, that 'systematic, unbroken executive practice' can constitutionally gloss executive power. The problem for the President in Youngstown is that Frankfurter found only three isolated instances — not enough. But in a hypothetical with a longer, more consistent history of seizures and clear congressional acquiescence, this argument could prevail. The lesson is that Category Three is not automatically fatal — the President can still win with an exclusive, preclusive power (as in Zivotofsky) or potentially with overwhelming historical practice.

title: The Legislative Veto Showdown
doctrineArea: Bicameralism and Presentment / Legislative Veto
description: The Attorney General has suspended the deportation of a foreign national who overstayed his visa, finding he meets all statutory criteria: continuous U.S. residence for over seven years, good moral character, and extreme hardship if deported. Under the statute, either house of Congress can vote by simple majority to override the Attorney General's decision and require deportation — without involving the other house or the President.

steps.prompt: What is the fundamental constitutional defect in allowing one house of Congress to override the Attorney General's suspension of deportation?

choices.text: It violates the Due Process Clause because the foreign national has no opportunity to be heard before Congress votes.
choices.explanation: While due process concerns exist, that is not the holding. The Court's analysis focused on the structural requirements of Article I: when Congress takes action that is 'legislative in purpose and effect' — altering the legal rights, duties, and relations of persons outside the legislative branch — it must use bicameralism and presentment.

choices.text: It violates the requirements of bicameralism and presentment — Congress is acting legislatively without both houses voting and without presenting the action to the President for signature.
choices.explanation: Correct. The Court in INS v. Chadha held that the one-house veto was 'essentially legislative in purpose and effect' because it altered Chadha's legal rights and duties. The Constitution requires that all legislative action pass through both houses of Congress (bicameralism) and be presented to the President (presentment). The legislative veto satisfied neither requirement.

choices.text: It violates the Appointments Clause because Congress is effectively exercising executive power without proper authorization.
choices.explanation: This is not the basis for the holding. While there are separation-of-powers concerns, the Court's reasoning centered on Article I's bicameralism and presentment requirements, not the Appointments Clause. The test is whether Congress has acted legislatively — if so, it must follow the constitutionally prescribed process.

choices.text: It violates the Tenth Amendment by interfering with state deportation authority.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. Immigration is a federal power. The Tenth Amendment is not implicated here. The constitutional defect is entirely structural — Congress is exercising legislative power outside the required bicameralism-and-presentment process.

steps.prompt: If Congress wants to override the Attorney General's suspension of deportation, what is the constitutionally correct process?

choices.text: Both houses of Congress must pass a law requiring the deportation, and the President must sign it (or Congress must override a veto by two-thirds vote in each house).
choices.explanation: Correct. Under Chadha, when Congress wants to alter the legal rights and duties of persons outside the legislative branch, it must act through bicameralism and presentment. This means both houses must pass a bill and the President must sign it. This is the only constitutionally permissible method for Congress to exercise legislative power. The Congressional Review Act provides a model: both houses vote and the President signs.

choices.text: Both houses of Congress must vote to override, but no presidential involvement is required since this is internal congressional oversight.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. The Court treats unicameral and bicameral legislative vetoes identically — both are unconstitutional without presentment. Even if both houses vote, the action must still be presented to the President. Congressional 'oversight' that alters legal rights is legislative action requiring the full Article I process.

choices.text: A congressional committee can issue a binding resolution requiring deportation, as long as the committee has jurisdiction over immigration matters.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. A committee resolution is even further removed from bicameralism and presentment than a one-house vote. If a single house cannot exercise a legislative veto, a committee certainly cannot. Any action altering legal rights must go through the full legislative process.

choices.text: Congress can simply defund the Attorney General's office to force compliance — no formal legislative process is required for budget decisions.
choices.explanation: While Congress does control the budget, that is a separate power exercised through the normal appropriations process (which itself requires bicameralism and presentment). The question asks about the correct process for overriding a specific agency decision, and the answer is the full legislative process.

overallExplanation: INS v. Chadha (1983) struck down the legislative veto. The Court held that when Congress acts in a way that is 'legislative in purpose and effect' — specifically, when it alters 'the legal rights, duties, and relations of persons outside the legislative branch' — Congress must act through bicameralism and presentment. The one-house veto satisfied neither requirement. Chadha is a formalist opinion: the Court does not consider whether the legislative veto is convenient or efficient, only whether it complies with the Constitution's structural requirements. Justice White's functionalist dissent argued that if the Court allows Congress to delegate legislative power to agencies (a functionalist accommodation under the non-delegation doctrine), it should also allow Congress to retain a check through the legislative veto. Distinguishing the veto: report-and-wait provisions (agency notifies Congress, which may initiate normal legislation) are permissible. The Congressional Review Act (both houses plus presidential signature to void a regulation) is permissible because it uses bicameralism and presentment. A presidential cancellation power is not a legislative veto because Congress is not reserving veto authority for itself.
caseReferences: INS v. Chadha, 462 U.S. 919 (1983)
Article I, Section 7 (Bicameralism and Presentment)
Justice White's Functionalist Dissent
Congressional Review Act (5 U.S.C. §§ 801–808)
professorNote: Chadha is the paradigmatic formalist opinion. The Court does not ask whether the legislative veto is efficient, practical, or even necessary — only whether it complies with the Constitution's structural requirements. Justice White's functionalist dissent is equally important: if we allow Congress to delegate legislative power to agencies (a functionalist accommodation), it is inconsistent to forbid Congress from retaining a check on those agencies through the legislative veto. This formalism-functionalism tension runs through every separation-of-powers question in the course. On an exam, you should always identify which lens the Court is using and whether the opposing lens would produce a different result.
otherSide: Justice White's dissent offers the strongest counter-argument: the legislative veto is a practical necessity in the modern administrative state. Congress cannot write detailed legislation covering every regulatory question, so it delegates authority to agencies. The legislative veto was Congress's way of retaining democratic control over that delegated power — a 'check' in the checks-and-balances system. White argued it is deeply inconsistent for the Court to be functionalist about delegation (allowing it because government needs it) but formalist about the veto (striking it down on structural grounds). After Chadha eliminated the legislative veto, presidential power over agencies increased significantly — a trend continued by the removal-power decisions.

title: The Delegation Dilemma
doctrineArea: Non-Delegation Doctrine
description: Congress has passed the 'Public Wellness Protection Act,' creating a new federal agency — the U.S. Supplement Safety Agency (USSA). The statute's operative provision states only that the USSA 'shall issue rules and regulations concerning dietary supplements and vitamins.' There is no stated purpose, no standard to guide the agency's discretion, and no congressional findings in the statute. An industry group challenges the delegation as unconstitutional under Article I.

steps.prompt: Under the current non-delegation doctrine, what must Congress provide when delegating policymaking authority to an agency?

choices.text: Congress must draft the specific regulations itself and allow the agency only to enforce them.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. The non-delegation doctrine does not require Congress to write specific regulations. The entire premise of agency delegation is that Congress provides the policy direction while agencies fill in the details through their expertise. If Congress had to write every regulation, the administrative state could not function.

choices.text: Congress must provide an 'intelligible principle' to guide the agency's exercise of delegated authority.
choices.explanation: Correct. Under the intelligible principle test, originating from J.W. Hampton v. United States and applied since, Congress must provide an intelligible principle to guide agency discretion. The theory is that the intelligible principle itself is the legislative act, and everything the agency does afterward is executive — filling in the details. Almost anything has been found to qualify: 'in the public interest,' 'fair and equitable rates,' and 'regulate unreasonable risks' have all been upheld.

choices.text: Congress must retain a legislative veto over any regulations the agency produces.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. The legislative veto was struck down in INS v. Chadha as a violation of bicameralism and presentment. Congress cannot retain a legislative veto as a check on agency action. Other oversight tools — budget control, hearings, sunset provisions, and the Congressional Review Act — remain available.

choices.text: Congress must obtain the President's advance approval before delegating authority to any agency.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. There is no such requirement. Congress creates agencies and delegates authority through organic statutes, which are enacted through the normal legislative process (bicameralism and presentment, which includes presidential signature or veto override). No additional pre-delegation approval is needed.

steps.prompt: The statute says only that the USSA 'shall issue rules and regulations concerning dietary supplements and vitamins' — with no stated purpose or standard. Is this statute constitutional under the non-delegation doctrine?

choices.text: Yes, clearly constitutional — courts always find an intelligible principle, and this is no different from other upheld delegations like 'regulate in the public interest.'
choices.explanation: This is a defensible position given the doctrine's history, but it overstates the case. This statute arguably provides even less guidance than 'in the public interest,' because there is literally no operative standard at all — just 'go regulate.' While the Court has been extremely permissive, this represents the strongest possible case for a non-delegation violation. A more nuanced answer acknowledges the difficulty.

choices.text: Probably constitutional, but this is the strongest possible case for a non-delegation violation because the statute provides literally no guidance — not even a vague standard like 'public health and safety.'
choices.explanation: Correct. This statute is arguably the outer boundary of permissible delegation. While formulations like 'in the public interest' and 'to protect public health and safety' have been upheld, this statute provides no standard at all. The Court might look to legislative history, other statutory provisions, or congressional findings to supply a principle — but if none exist, this is the most likely statute to be struck down. Still, given the Court's extreme permissiveness, even this might survive.

choices.text: Clearly unconstitutional — this violates Schechter Poultry and Panama Refining because Congress has given no guidance whatsoever to the executive branch.
choices.explanation: This overstates the case in the other direction. While Schechter Poultry and Panama Refining (both 1935) did strike down statutes for lacking guidance, those cases have never been followed since. After 1937, the Court has never again struck down a statute on non-delegation grounds. The current doctrine is extremely permissive, and while there is a movement among conservative justices to reinvigorate the doctrine (see Gundy v. United States), it has not yet resulted in any statute being struck down.

choices.text: Unconstitutional because the major questions doctrine prohibits Congress from delegating significant policy decisions to agencies through vague statutory language.
choices.explanation: The major questions doctrine is related but distinct. It holds that courts should not assume Congress delegated 'massive power' to an agency through 'ambiguous little words.' This doctrine was applied in the tariffs case to reject an expansive reading of 'regulate importation' under IEEPA. But the major questions doctrine is about statutory interpretation — reading a statute narrowly — rather than striking down the delegation itself as unconstitutional.

overallExplanation: The non-delegation doctrine arises from Article I's grant of 'all legislative powers' to Congress. The argument is that when Congress delegates policymaking authority to agencies, it may be unconstitutionally giving away its legislative power. The doctrine's history mirrors the Commerce Clause: permissive for most of its existence, extremely strict in 1935 (Panama Refining and Schechter Poultry), then permissive again from 1937 onward. The current test is the intelligible principle test: Congress may delegate so long as it provides an 'intelligible principle' to guide agency discretion. Almost anything counts. However, there is a growing movement among conservative justices to reinvigorate the doctrine (Justice Gorsuch in Gundy v. United States). Justice Stevens offered an alternative theory: Congress can actually delegate legislative power, and there is nothing in the Constitution that says otherwise. The major questions doctrine adds a further limit on the interpretation side: courts should not read ambiguous statutory language to delegate massive new powers.
caseReferences: A.L.A. Schechter Poultry Corp. v. United States, 295 U.S. 495 (1935)
Panama Refining Co. v. Ryan, 293 U.S. 388 (1935)
J.W. Hampton, Jr., & Co. v. United States, 276 U.S. 394 (1928)
Gundy v. United States, 588 U.S. 128 (2019)
Article I, Section 1 ('All legislative Powers herein granted shall be vested in a Congress')
professorNote: The non-delegation doctrine is the sleeper issue in separation of powers. For ninety years, the intelligible principle test has been extraordinarily permissive — no statute has been struck down since 1935. But Justice Gorsuch's dissent in Gundy signals that at least four (and possibly five) justices want to reinvigorate the doctrine. The connection to the removal power is critical: Justice Kagan's argument in the Slaughter oral argument is that if Congress delegates massive power to agencies and the Court simultaneously strips Congress of tools to control those agencies (Chadha, Seila Law), the result is 'massive, unchecked, uncontrolled power' concentrated in the president. Whether non-delegation tightens may determine the answer to Kagan's concern.
otherSide: The strongest argument for reinvigorating the non-delegation doctrine comes from Justice Gorsuch in Gundy: the intelligible principle test has become so permissive that it is essentially no test at all. Congress routinely delegates enormous policymaking authority — telling agencies to regulate 'in the public interest' or to set 'fair and equitable' standards — with virtually no constraint. Gorsuch argues this violates Article I's grant of 'all legislative powers' to Congress and undermines democratic accountability. If unelected agency officials are making the major policy decisions, voters cannot hold anyone accountable. The counter-response (Justice Stevens's theory) is that the Constitution does not actually prohibit delegation of legislative power — it simply vests legislative power in Congress, and Congress chooses to exercise that power by delegating.

title: The Immunity Gauntlet
doctrineArea: Presidential Immunity / Trump v. United States
description: A former President is facing criminal charges related to three separate incidents: (1) issuing a pardon that prosecutors allege was exchanged for a financial payment; (2) pressuring a cabinet official to take a specific enforcement action during a phone call from the Oval Office; and (3) making statements at a private fundraising event for a political party that allegedly incited unlawful activity. You must analyze each incident under the Trump v. United States immunity framework.

steps.prompt: Incident 1: The allegedly corrupt pardon. Under the Trump v. United States framework, how should this presidential action be classified?

choices.text: Core presidential power with absolute immunity — the pardon power is conclusive and preclusive, and courts may not inquire into the President's motives.
choices.explanation: Correct. The pardon power is the paradigmatic example of a core presidential power — it is conclusive (final, unreviewable) and preclusive (no other branch shares this power). Under Trump v. United States, core powers receive absolute immunity. Critically, the Court held that courts may not inquire into the President's motives when classifying acts as official or unofficial. Even if the pardon was allegedly exchanged for money, the action of pardoning is itself a core power, and the no-motive-inquiry rule prevents using the alleged corrupt purpose to reclassify it as unofficial.

choices.text: Unofficial conduct with no immunity — accepting money for a pardon is bribery, which is inherently unofficial.
choices.explanation: This reflects an intuitive reaction but is incorrect under the framework. The Court explicitly held that courts may not inquire into presidential motives. The classification depends on what the President was doing (issuing a pardon — a core power), not why the President was doing it. As the Court stated, allowing motive inquiries 'would risk exposing even the most obvious instances of official conduct to judicial examination on the mere allegation of improper purpose.'

choices.text: Shared/twilight-zone power with presumptive immunity — pardons involve the executive and judicial branches.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. While pardons affect judicial proceedings, the pardon power is exclusively presidential. It is not shared with Congress or the judiciary. The power is specifically enumerated in Article II and has been understood as essentially absolute and unlimited. This places it squarely in the core powers category, not the shared/twilight zone.

steps.prompt: Incident 2: The Oval Office phone call pressuring a cabinet official. What level of immunity applies?

choices.text: Core presidential power — directing cabinet officials is part of the President's exclusive authority as head of the executive branch.
choices.explanation: This is arguable but may overstate the classification. The Court in Trump v. United States listed the power to control the investigation and prosecution of federal crimes and the removal power as core powers. Directing a cabinet official on enforcement could be seen as core (head of the executive branch) or as within the 'outer perimeter' of official duties, which receives at least presumptive immunity. The better answer recognizes this falls at least within the outer perimeter.

choices.text: At least presumptive immunity — directing enforcement actions falls within the outer perimeter of official presidential responsibilities, and the government must show 'no dangers of intrusion on executive authority' to overcome it.
choices.explanation: Correct. Communicating with cabinet officials about enforcement falls comfortably within the 'outer perimeter of official responsibilities.' The Court held that the President receives at least presumptive immunity for such acts — the government must show that 'applying a criminal prohibition to that act would pose no dangers of intrusion on the authority and functions of the executive branch.' This is an extremely difficult standard for prosecutors to meet. There is also a strong argument this reaches core-power status, but presumptive immunity is the floor.

choices.text: No immunity — pressuring officials to take specific enforcement actions is unofficial conduct that goes beyond legitimate presidential functions.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. The President communicating with executive branch officials about enforcement policy is quintessentially official conduct. The framework distinguishes between what the President is doing (communicating with a subordinate about official business) and the alleged illegality of the action. Even if the specific directive was improper, the act of directing a cabinet official is official.

steps.prompt: Incident 3: Statements at a private political fundraiser. Considering the framework, can the former President be prosecuted for these statements?

choices.text: Yes — speaking at a private party fundraiser is unofficial conduct as a candidate or party leader, and unofficial acts receive no immunity.
choices.explanation: Correct. The Court acknowledged that 'there may be contexts in which the President speaks in an unofficial capacity — perhaps as a candidate for office or party leader.' A private political fundraiser is the strongest case for unofficial conduct. However, even here, prosecutors face a significant hurdle: the evidence-exclusion rule prevents using evidence from official acts to prosecute unofficial acts. If any official communications or actions are intertwined with the fundraiser statements, that evidence may be excluded.

choices.text: No — most presidential communications fall within the outer perimeter of official responsibilities, and the President's speech at any event is presumptively official.
choices.explanation: This is a plausible argument given the Court's broad language that 'most of a President's public communications are likely to fall comfortably within the outer perimeter of his official responsibilities.' However, the Court specifically identified 'candidate for office or party leader' as contexts where unofficial capacity may apply. A private fundraiser for a political party is precisely that kind of context. The analysis must be 'fact-specific' and examine 'content, form, and context.'

choices.text: It depends — but even if unofficial, the evidence-exclusion rule may prevent prosecutors from building a case if official-act evidence is needed to prove the charges.
choices.explanation: This is an excellent observation about a practical barrier. The Court held that the government cannot use evidence from official acts to prosecute unofficial acts. If building the case against the President for the fundraiser statements requires using evidence from official communications, meetings, or directives, that evidence is excluded. This rule substantially limits the government's ability to prosecute even clearly unofficial conduct. However, the threshold question of classification should be answered first.

overallExplanation: Trump v. United States (2024) created a three-tier immunity framework for former presidents facing criminal prosecution. Tier 1 — Core Presidential Powers: absolute immunity. Core powers are 'conclusive and preclusive' — pardons, investigation/prosecution of federal crimes, and removal of officers. Tier 2 — Shared/Twilight-Zone Powers: at least presumptive immunity, potentially absolute. The government must show that 'applying a criminal prohibition would pose no dangers of intrusion on the authority and functions of the executive branch.' Tier 3 — Unofficial Acts: no immunity for actions 'manifestly or palpably beyond' presidential authority. Two critical additional rules substantially broaden protection: (1) Courts may not inquire into presidential motives — even an allegedly corrupt motive cannot transform an official act into an unofficial one. (2) Evidence from official acts cannot be used to prosecute unofficial acts — limiting the government's ability to build cases even for clearly unofficial conduct. Justice Sotomayor's dissent argued that the Constitution says nothing about presidential immunity (unlike the Speech and Debate Clause for Congress), that the majority ignores the rule-of-law side of the balance, and that the space for truly 'unofficial' acts may be vanishingly small.
caseReferences: Trump v. United States, 603 U.S. ___ (2024)
Nixon v. Fitzgerald, 457 U.S. 731 (1982)
Clinton v. Jones, 520 U.S. 681 (1997)
Justice Sotomayor's Dissent (Rule of Law and Vanishingly Small Unofficial Space)
professorNote: The immunity framework from Trump v. United States is best understood through the two additional rules that dramatically expand its reach: (1) courts may not inquire into presidential motives, and (2) evidence from official acts cannot be used to prosecute unofficial acts. Together, these rules may make the 'unofficial acts' category far smaller than it appears on a diagram. On an exam, always identify the category first (core / shared / unofficial), then address the two additional rules. The hardest cases involve presidential speech — the Court acknowledged that most public communications fall within the outer perimeter of official duties, but carved out an exception for the president acting 'as a candidate for office or party leader.' That line is deliberately left blurry.
otherSide: Justice Sotomayor's dissent provides the strongest counter-framework. First, the Constitution says nothing about presidential immunity — unlike the Speech and Debate Clause, which explicitly protects members of Congress. The majority derives its framework entirely from structural inference. Second, the majority ignores the criminal-justice side of the balance that was central to United States v. Nixon and Trump v. Vance — the rule of law and the principle that no person is above the law. Third, the 'no dangers' standard for overcoming presumptive immunity and the evidence-exclusion rule may make the space for truly unofficial acts 'vanishingly small.' As Sotomayor asked: is it really such a bad thing for a president to think twice before taking an action, when that action might be criminal?

title: The Federal Funding Squeeze
doctrineArea: Spending Clause / Coercion Doctrine
description: The federal government currently provides states with $800 million annually under the 'Safe Roads Program' for highway improvements. Congress now passes the 'National Public Health Initiative Act,' which provides $50 million in new grants to states for vaccination outreach. The Act also includes a provision stating: any state that does not implement the vaccination program will lose not only the $50 million in new health funds but also 40% of its existing Safe Roads Program funding.

steps.prompt: Under the Spending Clause framework from NFIB v. Sebelius, the first question is whether this is a 'one program' situation (Scenario A) or a 'two programs' situation (Scenario B). Which is it?

choices.text: Scenario A (one program) — Congress is simply placing conditions on the use of granted funds, so no further analysis is needed.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. Scenario A applies when the federal government grants money and requires states to use it for specified purposes within the same program. Here, Congress is threatening funding from a completely separate program (Safe Roads) to coerce compliance with the new health initiative. That makes this a two-program situation requiring coercion and germaneness analysis.

choices.text: Scenario B (two programs) — the vaccination conditions threaten funding from a separate, pre-existing highway program, triggering both the germaneness and coercion analyses.
choices.explanation: Correct. When the federal government conditions funding from one program on compliance with a completely separate program's requirements, this is a Scenario B situation under South Dakota v. Dole and NFIB v. Sebelius. The Safe Roads Program and the National Public Health Initiative are distinct programs with different purposes, different funding streams, and different conditions. Both germaneness and coercion must be analyzed.

choices.text: Neither — the Spending Clause does not apply because Congress is regulating public health, not spending.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. Congress is exercising its spending power by conditioning federal funds. The Spending Clause analysis applies whenever the federal government places conditions on grants to states. The subject matter (public health versus highways) is relevant to the germaneness analysis, but the Spending Clause framework clearly applies.

steps.prompt: Applying the Sebelius framework, evaluate whether the condition — losing 40% of Safe Roads funding for non-compliance with a vaccination program — is constitutional.

choices.text: Constitutional — 40% of highway funds is less than the 10% of total state budgets found coercive in Sebelius, so it passes the coercion test.
choices.explanation: This misapplies the coercion analysis. The question is not just about the percentage — it is about whether the state has a realistic choice to decline. Losing 40% of a major funding program ($320 million of $800 million) is a massive financial penalty. Moreover, the germaneness requirement independently poses a fatal problem: vaccination outreach is not related to highway improvement. The condition fails on germaneness alone.

choices.text: Unconstitutional — it fails both the germaneness test (vaccination is unrelated to highways) and likely the coercion test ($320 million is a 'gun to the head' rather than gentle encouragement).
choices.explanation: Correct. This condition fails on two independent grounds. First, germaneness: requiring vaccination compliance to retain highway funds is not germane — the condition has no relationship to the original Safe Roads Program. Justice O'Connor's concurrence in Dole warned against allowing the federal government to impose unrelated conditions, comparing it to requiring states to 'move the state capital' to keep highway money. Second, coercion: $320 million is likely 'a gun to the head' rather than 'a relatively mild encouragement.' While we know 5% of highway funds is not coercive (Dole) and 10% of an entire state budget is coercive (Sebelius), 40% of a major program sits well on the coercive side.

choices.text: Constitutional — under South Dakota v. Dole, Congress has broad authority to condition federal funds, and the drinking-age-to-highway-funds link was upheld despite being arguably tangential.
choices.explanation: This overstates Dole. In Dole, the Court upheld a relatively modest condition (5% of highway funds) with at least an arguable connection between the drinking age and highway safety. Here, the connection between vaccination and highway funding is far more attenuated — virtually nonexistent — and the amount at risk (40% of the program) is dramatically larger. Sebelius established that there are real limits on conditional spending, and this scenario exceeds them.

choices.text: Constitutional — Congress could have repealed the Safe Roads Program entirely and re-passed it with the new vaccination condition, so this is really just one program.
choices.explanation: This invokes Justice Ginsburg's argument from her Sebelius dissent — that Congress could theoretically repeal and re-enact the entire program. But the Chief Justice's plurality rejected this reasoning, finding the Medicaid expansion was 'transformational' and 'different in kind.' Here, adding vaccination requirements to a highway program is even more clearly a separate program than the Medicaid expansion was. Ginsburg's position did not command a majority.

overallExplanation: The Spending Clause permits Congress to condition federal funds to advance federal policy. Issues arise when conditions become coercive or unrelated to the funding program. Under NFIB v. Sebelius, the analysis depends on whether the arrangement involves one program (Scenario A — conditions on use of the same funds, generally permissible without further analysis) or two programs (Scenario B — conditions linking separate programs, requiring germaneness and coercion analysis). Germaneness requires the condition be related to the federal program. Coercion asks whether the financial pressure is so great that the state has no real choice — a 'gun to the head' rather than 'a relatively mild encouragement.' From Dole, we know 5% of highway funds is not coercive. From Sebelius, we know 10% of an entire state budget is coercive. Between those benchmarks lies significant uncertainty. The underlying concerns are state sovereignty and democratic accountability — if the federal government can effectively force states to adopt federal policy through spending conditions, citizens cannot determine whom to blame for unpopular policies.
caseReferences: NFIB v. Sebelius, 567 U.S. 519 (2012)
South Dakota v. Dole, 483 U.S. 203 (1987)
Justice Ginsburg's Dissent in Sebelius (One-Program Argument)
Justice O'Connor's Concurrence in Dole (Germaneness Requirement)
professorNote: The one-program versus two-program distinction is the threshold question in every Spending Clause problem. If it is one program (Scenario A), at least five justices in Sebelius say no further analysis is required. If it is two programs (Scenario B), you must run both the germaneness and coercion tests. The coercion benchmarks are rough: 5% of highway funds is not coercive (Dole); 10% of the entire state budget is coercive (Sebelius). Everything between those poles is uncertain. On an exam, identify the scenario type first, then flag the underlying policy concerns — state sovereignty and democratic accountability. If the federal government can effectively force states to adopt federal policy through overwhelming financial pressure, citizens cannot determine whom to blame.
otherSide: Justice Ginsburg's dissent in Sebelius offers the strongest argument against the coercion holding: Congress could have repealed the original Medicaid program entirely and re-passed it with the expansion included — making it indisputably one program. The distinction between 'modifying' an existing program and 'creating' a new one is artificial. If Congress has the greater power (repeal and replace), it should have the lesser power (amend in place). Moreover, the 'coercion' concept is inherently subjective — at what dollar amount does an offer become a threat? The majority provides only two data points (5% and 10%) and no principled way to draw the line between them. The counter-response is that the Chief Justice's 'gun to the head' metaphor captures a real constitutional concern: when federal spending constitutes such a large share of a state's budget that refusal is economically impossible, the state's 'choice' is illusory.

precedentConditions.descriptionAppend: Precedent in play: because you previously tolerated broad congressional delegations, the federal government argues Congress should also enjoy wide latitude when structuring national programs through conditional spending.

stepModifications.textAppend: The government explicitly analogizes broad spending flexibility to broad delegation flexibility.

precedentConditions.descriptionAppend: Precedent in play: your earlier non-delegation ruling insisted that Congress must speak clearly when it wants major policy change. The states now argue that the same clarity-and-accountability concern cuts against coercive funding threats.

stepModifications.textAppend: The states say this is another attempt to shift major policy responsibility without owning it directly.

title: The Frozen Accounts
doctrineArea: Youngstown Zone 2 / Frankfurter Historical Practice Test
description: Citing a national security emergency, the President orders the Treasury Department to freeze the bank accounts of foreign nationals suspected of financing hostile operations against the United States. No federal statute either authorizes or prohibits this action. Presidents have taken similar actions four times over the past century, but in 1984 the House passed a narrow resolution disapproving of the most recent seizure.

steps.prompt: The Office of Legal Counsel has confirmed there is no statute authorizing or prohibiting the President's action. Under Justice Jackson's Youngstown framework, which category best describes the President's action?

choices.text: Category Two (Zone of Twilight) — the President acts in the absence of any congressional grant or denial of authority, relying on independent presidential powers.
choices.explanation: Correct. When no statute either authorizes or prohibits the President's action, the case falls in Jackson's Category Two — the 'zone of twilight' where 'congressional inertia, indifference, or quiescence may sometimes enable, if not invite, measures on independent presidential responsibility.' The resolution of the President's power will depend on 'the imperatives of events and contemporary imponderables.' This is a rare true Zone Two case.

choices.text: Category Three (Lowest Ebb) — the 1984 House resolution disapproving of a prior seizure means the President acts contrary to Congress's will.
choices.explanation: Partially credited. The 1984 House resolution is relevant evidence, but a resolution passed by only one chamber is not a statute and does not clearly express Congress's will in the way Category Three requires. Category Three applies when the President acts 'incompatible with the expressed or implied will of Congress.' A single-chamber resolution is probative but insufficient to place this squarely in Category Three. This is better analyzed as a Zone Two case with the resolution factored into the Frankfurter practice analysis.

choices.text: Category One (Zenith) — the President's inherent Commander in Chief power, combined with historical practice, puts the action at its maximum authority.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. Category One requires that the President act 'pursuant to an express or implied authorization of Congress.' There is no congressional statute authorizing this seizure. The Commander in Chief power and historical practice may be relevant to the analysis, but they do not place the action in Category One. Jackson was clear that Category One requires congressional authorization, not merely presidential assertion of inherent power.

steps.prompt: In the Zone of Twilight, the President's authority depends partly on historical practice as constitutional gloss. Under Justice Frankfurter's concurrence, evaluate the President's practice-based claim. Consider the factors: number, scope, duration, contemporary legal justification, and congressional acquiescence.

choices.text: The practice is insufficient. Four instances over a hundred years lack the frequency and consistency needed, and the 1984 House resolution undermines the acquiescence factor. This does not constitute the 'systematic, unbroken executive practice' that Frankfurter requires.
choices.explanation: Correct. Frankfurter's standard requires 'a systematic, unbroken executive practice, long pursued to the knowledge of Congress but never before questioned.' Four instances over a century are sporadic, not systematic. In Youngstown itself, Frankfurter concluded that 'three isolated instances do not add up' to sufficient number, scope, or duration. Here, four isolated instances are similarly insufficient. Moreover, the 1984 House resolution breaks the pattern of congressional acquiescence. Though only a single-chamber resolution, it demonstrates that Congress has questioned the practice, weakening the acquiescence element.

choices.text: The practice is sufficient. Four instances establish a pattern, and three of the four occurred without any congressional objection, creating substantial acquiescence that glosses the President's executive power.
choices.explanation: Partially credited. You correctly identify that three of the four instances drew no congressional response, which supports acquiescence. However, Frankfurter's factors require more than occasional use. The factors demand analysis of number (four is low), scope (were these identical actions?), duration (spread across a century, not a concentrated practice), and contemporary legal justification (what rationale did the presidents give?). Frankfurter found three instances insufficient in Youngstown. Four instances over a hundred years likely fall short, and the 1984 resolution breaks the acquiescence pattern.

choices.text: The practice analysis is irrelevant. In Zone Two, the President relies solely on inherent Article II powers — the Commander in Chief power and the Vesting Clause — not on historical practice.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. Frankfurter's historical practice analysis is directly relevant in Zone Two situations. Jackson himself noted that in the Zone of Twilight, 'congressional inertia, indifference, or quiescence may sometimes enable measures on independent presidential responsibility.' Frankfurter's practice test provides the analytical framework for evaluating exactly this kind of situation — whether a longstanding executive practice with congressional knowledge and acquiescence can serve as a constitutional gloss on presidential power. Ignoring this framework misses the primary analytical tool for Zone Two cases.

steps.prompt: Based on your Zone Two classification and Frankfurter practice analysis, issue your ruling on the constitutionality of the President's order to freeze the foreign nationals' bank accounts.

choices.text: The seizure is likely unconstitutional. The President's practice-based claim is too thin, the Commander in Chief and Vesting Clause do not independently support domestic asset seizures, and the 1984 disapproval resolution further weakens the President's position.
choices.explanation: Correct. In Zone Two, the President must rely on independent powers, and historical practice can serve as a gloss. But the practice here is insufficient under Frankfurter's factors. The Commander in Chief clause does not extend to domestic economic seizures — Jackson warned in Youngstown that stretching this power to domestic affairs based on a foreign conflict would be 'sinister and alarming.' The Vesting Clause alone does not supply substantive seizure authority. The Take Care clause requires laws to execute, and there are none. The President loses in Zone Two because neither the practice nor the independent constitutional powers support the action.

choices.text: The seizure is constitutional. Even though the practice is thin, the President's foreign affairs and national security powers provide sufficient independent authority to act in Zone Two, and Congress's failure to legislate amounts to acquiescence.
choices.explanation: Partially credited. The argument that foreign affairs powers may give the President broader latitude in Zone Two has some force — Curtis Wright's dicta about the President as 'sole organ' in foreign affairs is often cited. However, this involves domestic bank accounts held in U.S. banks, making it closer to a domestic seizure than a pure foreign affairs action. Congressional silence is not the same as acquiescence, and the 1984 resolution actively undermines that argument. The better analysis recognizes that the President's independent powers are insufficient here.

choices.text: The seizure is constitutional because the President's Article II powers are absolute in matters of national security, and no court may second-guess the President's determination that an emergency exists.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. The President does not have absolute power in national security matters. Jackson explicitly rejected the claim of inherent emergency powers in Youngstown, warning that the framers 'suspected that emergency powers would tend to kindle emergencies.' The Supreme Court has repeatedly held that presidential power, even in sensitive areas, is subject to judicial review. United States v. Nixon rejected absolute executive privilege; the Youngstown framework itself presupposes judicial evaluation of presidential action.

overallExplanation: This scenario applies the Youngstown framework and Frankfurter's historical practice test to a Zone Two case — the rarest and most analytically challenging of Jackson's categories. The key doctrinal points are: (1) When no statute addresses the President's action, the case falls in Zone Two; (2) Frankfurter's factors for evaluating historical practice as constitutional gloss — number, scope, duration, contemporary legal justification, and congressional acquiescence — provide the analytical framework; (3) Sporadic instances spread over a century do not constitute the 'systematic, unbroken executive practice' required; (4) A congressional disapproval resolution, even from one chamber, undermines the acquiescence element; and (5) Independent presidential powers like the Commander in Chief clause and the Vesting Clause do not extend to domestic economic seizures.
caseReferences: Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. v. Sawyer, 343 U.S. 579 (1952) (Jackson concurrence — Zone Two framework; Frankfurter concurrence — historical practice test)
Dames & Moore v. Regan, 453 U.S. 654 (1981) (Youngstown as a spectrum; 'general tenor' of congressional legislation can support presidential action)
Curtis Wright v. United States, 299 U.S. 304 (1936) (broad dicta on presidential foreign affairs power)

title: The Frozen Accounts: Statutory Twist
doctrineArea: Youngstown Zone 3 / Effect of a New Statute
description: The facts are the same as The Frozen Accounts — the President orders the Treasury to freeze bank accounts of foreign nationals suspected of financing hostile activities. However, in 2002, Congress passed the Foreign Accounts Security Act, which provides: 'The President may freeze the bank accounts of foreign nationals who have been deemed by a federal judge to pose a threat to national security.' The President has not sought any judicial determination and has acted unilaterally.

steps.prompt: The 2002 statute authorizes account seizures only after a federal judge makes a finding. The President has acted without any judicial determination. How does this statute change the Youngstown analysis compared to the no-statute scenario?

choices.text: The statute shifts the case from Zone Two to Zone Three. Congress has now spoken on this exact subject and required a judicial determination as a precondition. By acting without one, the President acts contrary to Congress's expressed will, placing the case at the President's lowest ebb of authority.
choices.explanation: Correct. The 2002 statute is a definitive congressional statement on when and how the President may freeze foreign nationals' accounts. Congress authorized the seizure power but conditioned it on a federal judge's finding. By acting without that judicial determination, the President is acting in tension with Congress's expressed will — Congress has said the President can do this, but only this way. This moves the case from Zone Two (no congressional signal) to Zone Three (contrary to Congress), where the President's power is at its lowest ebb. As the course materials explain, a new statute can move a situation from Zone Two to Zone Three.

choices.text: The statute actually helps the President, moving the case to Zone One. Congress has authorized account seizures, and the President is acting pursuant to that authorization.
choices.explanation: Partially credited but substantially incorrect. It is true that if the President had obtained a federal judge's determination and then frozen the accounts, this would be a Zone One case — Congress authorized the action, the President carried it out. But the President did not follow the statute's procedure. Congress authorized seizures only after a judicial finding. By bypassing that requirement, the President is not acting pursuant to Congress's authorization; the President is acting contrary to Congress's conditions. The conditional nature of the authorization is critical.

choices.text: The statute is irrelevant. The President has inherent Article II authority to protect national security, and Congress cannot condition the exercise of that power on a judicial finding.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. The premise that the President has inherent authority to freeze bank accounts in national security situations was already doubtful in the Zone Two analysis. Freezing domestic bank accounts is not a recognized inherent presidential power — it is not in the same category as exclusive, preclusive powers like the pardon or the recognition of foreign governments. Moreover, even if such a power existed, the statute demonstrates that Congress has addressed this exact subject, and the President's action is now measured against Congress's expressed will.

steps.prompt: In Zone Three, the President can still prevail if the President possesses an exclusive, preclusive constitutional power. Rule on the legality of the President's unilateral account freeze.

choices.text: The freeze is unconstitutional. The President has no exclusive, preclusive power to freeze domestic bank accounts. Freezing accounts is not akin to the pardon power or the recognition power. The Commander in Chief clause does not extend to domestic economic seizures, and the practice-based argument is even weaker now that Congress has definitively spoken.
choices.explanation: Correct. For the President to win in Zone Three, the President must point to an exclusive, preclusive constitutional power — one where only the President can act and Congress has no role. The only established example of a Zone Three presidential win is Zivotofsky, where the power to recognize foreign governments is exclusively presidential. Freezing bank accounts is nothing like that. Jackson rejected the Commander in Chief clause as a basis for domestic seizures in Youngstown. The Vesting Clause, standing alone, does not create substantive seizure authority. And the practice argument that might have had marginal force in Zone Two is now irrelevant — Congress has definitively spoken and required a judicial precondition. The President loses.

choices.text: The freeze is constitutional. The President's broad foreign affairs power, recognized in Curtis Wright, provides sufficient authority to prevail even in Zone Three when national security is at stake.
choices.explanation: Partially credited. Curtis Wright's dicta about broad presidential foreign affairs power is frequently cited, but it has limits. In Medellin, the Court held that the President's foreign affairs power, 'while strong, is not all-encompassing such that the president can do whatever he wants.' Medellin is a Zone Three case where the President lost despite invoking foreign affairs power. The President needs an exclusive, preclusive power to win in Zone Three, and the general foreign affairs power is not exclusive — Congress has substantial foreign affairs powers as well (declare war, regulate foreign commerce, control the purse). This is not like recognition of foreign governments in Zivotofsky.

choices.text: The freeze is constitutional because the Dames & Moore principle applies — the 'general tenor' of the statute supports the President's action even if the President did not follow the specific procedure.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. Dames & Moore involved a situation where no statute directly addressed the President's specific action — suspending claims — and the Court found the general tenor of related statutes supported the President in a Youngstown 1.5 situation. Here, the statute directly addresses this exact action and imposes a clear precondition. The 'general tenor' argument cannot override Congress's specific, express requirement of a judicial finding. As the tariffs case later confirmed, the Court rejected the Dames & Moore analogy when the statutory text was specific and clear. The statute does not support the President; it constrains the President.

overallExplanation: This scenario demonstrates how a statute can shift a Youngstown analysis from Zone Two to Zone Three, dramatically worsening the President's legal position. The key doctrinal points are: (1) When Congress passes a statute addressing the exact subject of the President's action and the President fails to comply with its conditions, the case moves to Zone Three; (2) In Zone Three, the President can only win by invoking an exclusive, preclusive constitutional power — such as the pardon power (Youngstown) or the recognition power (Zivotofsky); (3) Freezing bank accounts is not an exclusive presidential power; (4) The Dames & Moore 'general tenor' doctrine does not apply when Congress has specifically legislated on the subject; (5) The practice-based argument from Frankfurter loses its force once Congress has definitively spoken.
caseReferences: Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. v. Sawyer, 343 U.S. 579 (1952) (Category Three — lowest ebb of presidential power)
Zivotofsky v. Kerry, 576 U.S. 1 (2015) (President wins in Zone Three on exclusive recognition power)
Medellin v. Texas, 552 U.S. 491 (2008) (President loses in Zone Three — foreign affairs power has limits)
Dames & Moore v. Regan, 453 U.S. 654 (1981) ('general tenor' doctrine inapplicable when Congress has spoken specifically)

title: The Tariff Gambit
doctrineArea: IEEPA / Major Questions Doctrine / Statutory Interpretation
description: The President declares an economic emergency and imposes sweeping tariffs on foreign goods, claiming authority under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). IEEPA authorizes the President, during a declared emergency, to 'investigate, block during the pendency of investigation, regulate, or prohibit' the importation of certain property. The President argues that the power to 'regulate importation' includes the power to impose tariffs. The government concedes the President has no inherent power to tax.

steps.prompt: The entire case turns on whether IEEPA's authorization to 'regulate importation' encompasses the power to impose tariffs. Evaluate the statutory interpretation question.

choices.text: 'Regulate' and 'tax' are different concepts. Congress knew how to grant tariff authority — other statutes specifically authorize tariffs with procedural and substantive limitations — but chose not to do so in IEEPA. The statutory text does not support the claimed power.
choices.explanation: Correct. Chief Justice Roberts made three straightforward statutory interpretation arguments. First, 'regulate' and 'tax' are different things — a common-sense understanding. Second, Congress has specifically authorized tariffs in other statutes (with procedural and substantive safeguards), demonstrating that Congress knows how to grant tariff power when it wants to. The fact that Congress gave the President specific tariff authority elsewhere but not in IEEPA suggests an intentional omission. Third, the President chose IEEPA precisely because it lacked the limitations of other tariff statutes, which confirms IEEPA was never meant to authorize tariffs.

choices.text: 'Regulate importation' is broad enough to include tariffs. Tariffs are a form of regulating imports because they affect what can enter the country and at what cost. Under Dames & Moore, the 'general tenor' of IEEPA's broad grant supports the President.
choices.explanation: Partially credited. This mirrors Justice Kavanaugh's dissenting argument, which has analytical force. Kavanaugh argued that IEEPA's broad language and the Dames & Moore precedent supported the President. However, the majority rejected the Dames & Moore analogy. Roberts wrote that Dames & Moore was 'exceedingly narrow,' did not address tariff power, and 'if anything, highlights the importance of close attention to IEEPA's text.' The Court drew a sharp line between regulating importation (controlling what enters) and taxing importation (extracting revenue on entry). Six justices found the statutory text insufficient.

choices.text: The statutory interpretation question is irrelevant because the President has inherent authority under the Commander in Chief power and foreign affairs powers to impose tariffs during a national emergency.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. The government itself conceded that the President has no inherent power to tax. The power of taxation is given to Congress through Article I. Everyone agreed — both the majority and the dissent — that the President lacks inherent tariff authority. The entire case therefore turned on whether IEEPA, as a congressional statute, granted the specific power to impose tariffs. Arguments based on inherent presidential authority are foreclosed by the government's own concession and by basic constitutional structure.

steps.prompt: Several justices argue that the major questions doctrine applies. Under this doctrine, courts should not assume Congress delegated 'massive power' to an agency or the executive 'through ambiguous little words.' Does the major questions doctrine apply to the President's claimed tariff authority?

choices.text: Yes. Tariffs of this scale have vast economic significance — affecting the entire economy, consumer prices, and international trade. Reading 'regulate importation' to include sweeping tariff authority would constitute exactly the kind of massive delegation through ambiguous statutory language that the doctrine is designed to prevent.
choices.explanation: Correct. The major questions doctrine provides that courts should not read ambiguous statutory language to delegate massive new powers to agencies or the executive. Imposing sweeping tariffs on foreign goods has enormous economic consequences — it affects prices for every consumer, disrupts supply chains, and reshapes international trade relationships. Reading two words ('regulate importation') to authorize this kind of sweeping economic power is precisely the scenario the major questions doctrine addresses. The majority applied this principle to reinforce its statutory interpretation conclusion.

choices.text: No. The major questions doctrine applies only to agency delegations under statutes like the Clean Air Act, not to direct presidential action under a statute that specifically names the President as the actor.
choices.explanation: Partially credited. This raises a legitimate doctrinal question — most major questions cases have involved agency action, not direct presidential action. However, the Court applied the doctrine to the tariffs case, which involved the President acting directly under IEEPA. The underlying principle — that Congress does not hide elephants in mouseholes — applies regardless of whether the actor is an agency or the President. The doctrine is about statutory interpretation, not about who the actor is.

choices.text: No. IEEPA is a clear delegation of emergency power, and the major questions doctrine does not apply to emergency statutes where Congress deliberately grants broad discretion to the President.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. IEEPA does grant broad powers, but the question is whether those powers include the specific authority to impose tariffs. The major questions doctrine is relevant precisely because the claimed power is so sweeping. The breadth of the emergency context does not exempt the statute from ordinary principles of statutory interpretation. As the majority found, 'regulate importation' is not a clear statement of tariff authority, and emergency powers do not transform vague statutory language into a blank check.

steps.prompt: Thinking in Youngstown terms, issue your final ruling on the President's tariff authority under IEEPA.

choices.text: The tariffs are unconstitutional. This is a Youngstown Category Three case. IEEPA does not authorize tariffs, so the President is acting against Congress's implicit will. Since the President has no inherent power to tax, the President cannot win in Zone Three, and the tariffs must be struck down.
choices.explanation: Correct. The majority treated this as a Youngstown Category Three case. Congress did not give the President tariff authority through IEEPA, and Congress has specifically addressed tariff authority in other statutes with different procedures and limitations. The President chose IEEPA to avoid those limitations. Since the government conceded there is no inherent presidential power to tax, the President has no way to prevail in Zone Three. The tariffs are unauthorized by any statute or constitutional provision and must be struck down. This is a 6–3 decision.

choices.text: The tariffs are constitutional. This is a Youngstown Category One case — IEEPA gives the President the power to 'regulate importation,' which encompasses tariffs, putting the President at the zenith of authority.
choices.explanation: Partially credited. This reflects Justice Kavanaugh's dissenting position. Kavanaugh argued that 'regulate importation' encompassed tariff power, making this Category One. He also invoked the Dames & Moore precedent, arguing that if IEEPA supported claim suspension without express authorization, it should support tariffs. However, six justices disagreed. The majority held that 'regulate' and 'tax' are different concepts, that Congress knew how to authorize tariffs separately, and that the major questions doctrine counseled against reading this massive power into two ambiguous words.

choices.text: The tariffs are constitutional under a Youngstown Category 1.5 analysis. Even if IEEPA does not explicitly authorize tariffs, the 'general tenor' of IEEPA's broad emergency powers supports the President's action, just as the general tenor of statutes supported the President in Dames & Moore.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. The majority explicitly rejected the Dames & Moore analogy in one paragraph at the end of the opinion. Roberts wrote that Dames & Moore was 'exceedingly narrow,' did not address the power to regulate, and did not involve tariffs. 'If anything, that case highlights the importance of close attention to IEEPA's text.' The general tenor doctrine arose in a unique context — settlement of claims during a hostage crisis with a long presidential practice of similar action and congressional acquiescence. The tariff context is entirely different: Congress has specifically legislated on tariffs elsewhere and chose not to authorize them through IEEPA.

overallExplanation: This scenario tests statutory interpretation, the major questions doctrine, and the Youngstown framework in the context of presidential tariff authority. The key doctrinal points are: (1) The President has no inherent power to tax — taxation is an Article I congressional power; (2) IEEPA's authorization to 'regulate importation' does not encompass tariff power because 'regulate' and 'tax' are different concepts, Congress has separately authorized tariffs in other statutes, and the major questions doctrine counsels against reading massive power into ambiguous words; (3) The Dames & Moore 'general tenor' doctrine does not rescue the President when the text is clear and other statutes specifically address tariff authority; (4) In Youngstown terms, this is a Category Three case, and without inherent taxing power, the President cannot win.
caseReferences: Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. v. Sawyer, 343 U.S. 579 (1952)
Dames & Moore v. Regan, 453 U.S. 654 (1981) (general tenor doctrine — distinguished and rejected in tariffs context)
West Virginia v. EPA, 597 U.S. 697 (2022) (major questions doctrine)
IEEPA Tariffs Case (2025) (6–3 decision striking down tariffs imposed under IEEPA)

title: The Subpoenaed Tapes
doctrineArea: Executive Privilege / United States v. Nixon
description: A special prosecutor investigating potential criminal conduct within the executive branch subpoenas recordings of private Oval Office meetings between the President and senior advisors. The President moves to quash the subpoena, claiming an absolute executive privilege over all presidential communications. The President argues that disclosing these materials would chill candid advice from advisors and compromise the confidentiality essential to effective governance.

steps.prompt: The President claims that executive privilege is absolute — that no court can compel the production of confidential presidential communications under any circumstances. Is the President correct?

choices.text: No. Executive privilege is constitutionally based but not absolute. Because it is an implicit power — derived from the structure of Article II rather than explicitly stated — the Court applies a balancing test rather than a categorical rule.
choices.explanation: Correct. In United States v. Nixon, the Court recognized that the President has a constitutionally based privilege to keep communications confidential. The privilege derives from 'the supremacy of each branch within its own assigned area of constitutional duties' and from McCulloch v. Maryland's principle that incidental powers flow from enumerated powers. Two rationales support it: national security and facilitating open communication (analogous to attorney-client privilege). However, because the privilege is implicit — 'nowhere in the Constitution is there any explicit reference to a privilege of confidentiality' — the Court uses a balancing test. The key principle: explicit powers receive categorical protection; implicit powers are subject to interest balancing.

choices.text: Yes. The privilege is absolute because the separation of powers requires that each branch be supreme within its own sphere. Allowing courts to order production of presidential communications would violate that principle.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. The Court unanimously rejected the claim of absolute executive privilege in United States v. Nixon. While acknowledging the separation of powers principle, the Court held that the privilege is qualified, not absolute. The Constitution 'contemplates that practice will integrate the dispersed powers into a workable government. It enjoins upon its branches separateness but interdependence, autonomy but reciprocity.' An absolute privilege would place the President above the law in all circumstances, which is incompatible with the constitutional structure.

choices.text: No. Executive privilege does not exist at all because the Constitution does not mention it. The President has no basis to resist a lawful subpoena.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. The Court in United States v. Nixon recognized that executive privilege is constitutionally based even though it is not explicitly mentioned in the text. The privilege is 'implicit in the nature of the enumerated powers' and derives from the structural principle that each branch must have some zone of confidentiality to carry out its constitutional duties. Denying the existence of executive privilege entirely contradicts established precedent.

steps.prompt: Having determined that executive privilege is qualified, apply the balancing test. On one side is the President's interest in confidential communications; on the other is the special prosecutor's need for evidence in a criminal investigation. How should the balance be struck?

choices.text: The balance favors criminal justice. The 'general privilege of confidentiality' must yield to 'the fair administration of criminal justice.' The rule of law requires that relevant evidence be available for criminal proceedings. The materials must be produced, subject to in camera judicial review for national security concerns.
choices.explanation: Correct. The Court balanced 'the importance of the general privilege of confidentiality of presidential communications' against 'the inroads of such a privilege on the fair administration of criminal justice' and concluded that criminal justice prevails. The procedural safeguard is in camera review — the court examines the materials privately to screen for national security material before any disclosure. This balancing reflects the principle that in criminal cases, the weight on the criminal justice side is especially heavy because of the importance of the rule of law and the principle that no person is above the law. The President must produce the materials.

choices.text: The balance favors the President because the chilling effect on future presidential communications outweighs the prosecutor's need for these particular documents. The President should only be required to provide a privilege log describing the documents.
choices.explanation: Partially credited. The chilling effect on candid presidential communication is a legitimate and weighty concern — the Court acknowledged it as one of two core rationales for the privilege. However, in Nixon the Court concluded that in a criminal case, this interest yields to the administration of justice. The safeguard is not to deny the subpoena but to require in camera judicial review, which protects against unnecessary disclosure while ensuring the criminal process has access to needed evidence. In the civil context (as in Cheney v. District Court), the balance might come out differently.

choices.text: The balance is irrelevant because the special prosecutor is part of the executive branch, so this is an intra-branch dispute that the judiciary should not resolve.
choices.explanation: Incorrect. The Court in United States v. Nixon addressed and rejected this argument. Although the caption of the case — 'United States v. Nixon' — highlights the unusual posture of the executive branch effectively suing itself, the Court held that the judiciary must resolve the dispute. The special prosecutor had been granted specific authority and independence by regulation, and the Constitution vests the judicial power to decide cases in the courts. The intra-branch nature of the dispute does not deprive the courts of jurisdiction.

overallExplanation: This scenario applies the executive privilege doctrine from United States v. Nixon. The key doctrinal points are: (1) Executive privilege is constitutionally based but implicit — nowhere mentioned in the text; (2) Because it is implicit, not explicit, the Court applies a balancing test rather than a categorical rule; (3) Two rationales support the privilege: national security and facilitating open communication; (4) In criminal cases, the balance favors the administration of justice — 'the general privilege of confidentiality' yields to 'the fair administration of criminal justice'; (5) In camera review is the procedural safeguard; (6) The key principle: explicit powers (pardon) get categorical protection; implicit powers (executive privilege) get interest balancing.
